title: Unit 5: The Accusative Case and Prepositions - Grammar Podcast

script.text: In Unit 5, we explore the Accusative Case, which is the primary tool for identifying the direct object of a sentence. In Modern Greek, when a noun is the recipient of an action, its morphological structure must shift. Athina, please reiterate the transformation rules for each gender.
Στα αρσενικά, το άρθρο 'ο' γίνεται 'τον' και χάνουμε το τελικό 'ς'. Στα θηλυκά, το άρθρο 'η' γίνεται 'την'. Τα ουδέτερα παραμένουν ίδια.

It is critical to observe the 'Masculine Nu' rule: in formal writing, we always retain the final 'N' in the masculine article 'ton' to distinguish it from the neuter 'to'. Furthermore, we must address the 'Mirror Exception' regarding the verb 'Eimai'. Athina?
Το ρήμα 'είμαι' δεν χρησιμοποιεί αιτιατική. Λέμε 'Είμαι ο Γιώργος', όχι 'τον Γιώργο'.

Next, we examine the three fundamental prepositions: 'Se', 'Apo', and 'Me'. These prepositions govern the Accusative Case. Pay special attention to 'Se', which frequently merges with the definite article to form a single phonetic unit. Athina, please demonstrate the 'Se' combinations.
Στον, στην, στο. Για παράδειγμα: Πάω στο σινεμά. Πάω στην Αθήνα.

Finally, we must master the elision of the feminine 'nu'. This depends entirely on the succeeding phonetic environment. We retain the 'nu' before vowels and hard consonants, while dropping it before soft, continuous sounds. Athina, give us a clear example.

This concludes our academic overview of Unit 5. A robust understanding of these case transformations and prepositional rules is required for accurate Greek syntax. Proceed to the practice exercises to reinforce these concepts.
